title: Craig Medred: Riding the tide to Kenai: Don't try this alone
summary: As Jeff Webster motored the Seward Highway from Portage north toward Anchorage, he would stare at the brown waters of Turnagain Arm and dream of what a grand adventure it would be to ride a driftboat on the outgoing tide all the way back to his home in Kenai. Friends he told of the idea thought it was nuts, having heard many stories about the dangers of the Turnagain mud and tides. "But it's not that crazy," the 46-year-old Kenai fishing guide insisted. "My ancestors came across the Oregon Trail. So, I'm thinking, if I can't float a boat from the Placer River down to the Kenai River, I must be adopted." Webster is a man of big emotions that sometimes get the better of him. Some will recognize his name from a nasty little encounter with antiwar protesters in Soldotna two years ago. Webster,…

title: Two die in McKinley fall
summary: ANCHORAGE -- Twin brothers who likely reached the summit of North Americas highest mountain died in a fall, a Denali National Park and Preserve official said Wednesday. It is believed that 55-year-old brothers Jerry and Terry Humphrey of Negley, Ohio, reached the Mount McKinley summit and were descending when one of them fell, perhaps falling into the other, said spokeswoman Maureen McLaughlin. The deaths were the first of the 2005 climbing season on the 20,320-foot mountain in central Alaska. One person died on McKinley last year after being hit by a falling rock. The brothers were not roped together, McLaughlin said. The bodies were found at 17,300 feet, just below Denali Pass. Since 1980, the steep Denali Pass traverse has been the site of six previous deadly falls, McLaughlin said. Th…

title: Craig Medred: Airport security more formidable than McKinley
summary: Training hard in the Bavarian Alps with the dream of tackling Mount McKinley, climbers Claudia Bechhein and Harold Lindner never thought that just getting to the base of the mountain could prove to be such a challenge. They were prepared for rocks, ice and storms, and they climbed in awful European winter to ready themselves for the cold on the West Buttress route to the 20,320-foot summit of North America. They never gave much thought to how hard it might be to get from Germany to Alaska across post-9/11 America. But at the Denver airport, they were introduced to the realities of U.S. travel in 2005. "We ... had to bring our luggage through customs (and) there wasn't enough time,'' said Bechhein, 35. America, as any American who has tried to get back into the country in the past year know…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Sorlie to visit Alaska this weekend Daily News staff Published: May 19th, 2005 Last Modified: May 19th, 2005 at 01:47 PM Two-time Iditarod winner Robert Sorlie returns to Alaska this weekend to collect his rings and his accolades. Chas St. George of the Iditarod Trail Committee announced Thursday that Sorlie from 1 p.m. to 4 p.m. Sunday will be at the ITC headquarters, Mile 2.2, Knik-Goose Bay Road, in the Matanuska-Susitna Borough. Sorlie will be available to sign memorabilia, as well, and will be talking about future races, said St. George. He said Sorlie would also pick up championship rings for the two years in which he won the race. Sorlie, a Norwegian, and his eight dogs finished the 1,100-mile sled-dog race on March 16. It took the 2003 winner nine days, 18 hours and 39 minutes to c…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Why old and heavy is worth replacing with new and light Published: April 24th, 2005 Last Modified: April 25th, 2005 at 05:57 AM Is your outdoor gear overbuilt? Let's do a quick inventory. How many pieces of equipment do you own in triplicate, quadruplicate, quintuplicate? How many packs, pile jackets, Gore-Tex parkas, fishing reels, tents, snowshoes, skis or other stuff does one person need? Why do you have all this stuff? Well, if you're like many of us, it's because in our technology-driven, rapidly evolving world, better stuff came along that you had to have. Just had to! But you couldn't bring yourself to throw out the old stuff. Why not? Because it had a lot of life left in it. So now, ask yourself again, is your outdoor gear overbuilt? The question is particularly timel…

title: Local venues offer red-hot spring birding
summary: The songs you're hearing outside your window every morning are a clear sign birding season is upon us. Songbirds are arriving, ducks have returned, shorebirds are everywhere. And now is the time to get out and watch. Sirena Brownlee, biologist and Anchorage Audubon Society field trip leader, said now is a particularly good time for beginner birders to take up the activity because there is such diversity in the forests and lakes. "The birds are going crazy out in Potter Marsh, and out at Westchester Lagoon for waterfowl and shorebirds and gulls," she said. "And then out at some of the local lakes you have a good chance of seeing loons right now." Taking up birding is not difficult, Brownlee said. It helps to have the expertise of local guides when you're starting out, but after a few sessio…

title: 1 of 1 Explore the Eagle River greenbelt with a hike sponsored by the Eagle River Nature Center at 11 a.m. May 15. Volun…
summary: 1 of 1 Explore the Eagle River greenbelt with a hike sponsored by the Eagle River Nature Center at 11 a.m. May 15. Volunteer Liza Sanden will lead an all-ages hike on a 1.5-mile trail along Southfork Falls to the confluence. Meet at Starbucks inside the Carrs store in Eagle River. The hike is limited to the first 12 people who register by calling 694-2108. Please provide a contact phone number and e-mail at the time of registration. Rodak Trail is pictured above. EVAN R. STEINHAUSER / Daily News archive 2004

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: COMMUNITY EVENTS Make Campbell clean with Sierra Club group The Knik Group of the Sierra Club is adopting Campbell Creek Park, near Lake Otis Parkway and Tudor Road, and is hosting its first community cleanup 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. Saturday. The event is being held in conjunction with Anchorage's Creek Cleanup Day. Come on down to help clean up this area of Campbell Creek. Trash bags, gloves, snacks and drinks will be provided. Bring a picnic lunch and meet at the park. For information or to sign up, contact outing leader Nadine at nadine.dunbar@alaska.sierra club.org or call 276-4060. FISHING Practice knots, casts with fly-fishing group Pudge Kleinkauf of Women's Flyfishing offers "Refresher Knot Tying and Fly Casting" classes 1 to 4:30 p.m. today and again June 19 for fly-fishers who want to …

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: BIRDING Audubon group hosts morning bird walks Join Anchorage Audubon for weekly walks to celebrate the return of songbirds who visit the boreal forests of Anchorage to refuel and nest. The walks are set for 6 a.m. Thursday, and May 19 and 26 beginning at the parking lot at the Campbell Creek Science Center, 6881 Abbott Loop Road. The first two trips will be led by Bruce Seppi, wildlife biologist with the Bureau of Land Management. The last two trips will be led by Sirena Brownlee, biologist and Anchorage Audubon Society field trip leader. Trips will last one to two hours. For information, call 222-7905. Kenai Shorebird event to be held this weekend The Kenai Shorebird Celebration and Workshop is 1 to 7 p.m. Friday and 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. Saturday at the Kenai River Flats. This two-day celeb…

title: Finding the right salmon or halibut charter
summary: Most visitors to Southcentral do not have their own boats, meaning that if they want to go ocean fishing for salmon or halibut they're going to have to hire a charter. The good news is that, from Cordova and Valdez north through Whittier and on around to the Kenai Peninsula, there is no shortage of charter boats available. When the fishing is red hot, you can catch lots of fish regardless. It's when the fishing gets tough -- and, yes, even in Alaska there are days when the bite just doesn't turn on -- that choosing the right charter can make the difference between a day of sightseeing and a day of reeling 'em in. The question then becomes, how do you choose the right charter for you? SLAB CITY Selecting a halibut charter is a bit different from choosing a charter for salmon fishing. That's…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Forest music for bird watchers The orchestra is flying in; groups watch and listen By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Anchorage Daily News Published: May 8th, 2005 Last Modified: May 8th, 2005 at 02:58 AM The sun was barely up when 30 people crept as quietly as a small mob could into the woods near Campbell Creek. They were dressed in gear good for a January day, and it felt like winter, but it was just the overnight chill hanging on. Among the 30 were an ex-school teacher, a biologist, two home-schooled kids and their parents, and some retired people. Their goal: To hear and spot Anchorage's early migrating songbirds and check out other birds that stay here year-round. Now is the time when hundreds of thousands of songbirds make an appearance in Southcentral Alaska. Every Thursday in May, bird walks s…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: The wicked truth about Gore-Tex running shoes Published: May 1st, 2005 Last Modified: May 4th, 2005 at 02:09 PM If one more outdoor magazine raves about a Gore-Tex lined trail running shoe, I'll be convinced the city boys who run those publications never leave town. This time it's Outside magazine's special 2005 Buy-Buy-Buy issue (officially the Buyer's Guide: Outside 2005 Annual) proclaiming the "Timberland Trail Lizard with Gore-Tex XCR" a "Trail Runner of the year." "Bonus,'' notes the accompanying copy. "Gore-Tex XCR uppers contribute to all-around environment-proofness.'' Now, I don't have a clue as to what "environment-proofness" is, but I'm guessing it's supposed to have something to do with protecting your feet from the environment. Either that or they're trying to say the shoe its…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Middle ground can be reached on foot and ATV RON WILMOT COMMENT Published: May 1st, 2005 Last Modified: May 1st, 2005 at 03:08 AM It's the great debate among backcountry enthusiasts: to motor or not to motor. Are you a muscle-power advocate who wouldn't dream of exploring the wilderness by machine, or are you smitten by the ability of snowmachines and ATVs to reach places inaccessible to people on foot, bikes or skis? While getting the two sides to shake hands might be about as easy as declaring peace in the Middle East, I think there's common ground. The two sides can coexist. Alaskans do it all the time. Both sides just need to let go of a few behaviors and try to empathize. The non-motorized group needs to get off its collective high horse and understand something: Riding a snowmachine …
